metadata.solution: Fix a positive integer $n < 1000$. A subset of $2n - 1$ cards is called \emph{good} if it contains a collection of $n$ consecutively numbered cards. To enumerate the good subsets, we categorize them into buckets $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_{2000}$. Let $B_k$ be the set of all subsets $S$ of $2n - 1$ cards such that: $ k-1 \notin S \quad \text{and} \quad k, k+1, \dots, k+n-1 \in S. $ Note that every element of $B_k$ is a good subset, and every good subset is an element of some bucket $B_k$. Furthermore, every subset is part of at most $1$ bucket because a subset of $2n - 1$ cards cannot contain $2$ disjoint collections of $n$ consecutively numbered cards. Therefore the probability of Cheshire winning is given by: $ P_n = \frac {\lvert B_1 \rvert + \lvert B_2 \rvert + \dots + \lvert B_{2000} \rvert} {\binom{2000}{2n - 1}} $ By the definitions of $B_k$ we also have that: $ B_k = \begin{cases} \binom{2000 - n}{n - 1} & \text{if $k = 1$} \\ \binom{2000 - n - 1}{n - 1} & \text{if $2 \leq k \leq 2000-n$} \\ 0 & \text{if...

input: Let $ABC$ be a triangle with $\angle B = 60\dg$ and $AB = 8$. Let $D$ be the foot of the altitude from $A$ to $BC$, and let $M$ be the midpoint of $CD$. If $AM = BM$, compute $AC^2$.

metadata.solution: Since $\angle ABD = 60\dg$ and $\angle ADB = 90\dg$, it follows that $\triangle ABD$ is a $30\dg$-$60\dg$-$90\dg$ triangle. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); pair B = (0, 0); pair A = (4, 4sqrt(3)); pair C = (12, 0); pair D = (4, 0); pair M = (8, 0); pair G = (A + B + C)/3; filldraw(A--B--C--cycle, opacity(0.4)+palered, darkred+1); draw(A--M, darkred+1); draw(A--D, black); label("$8$", midpoint(A--B), dir(150)); dot("$A$", A, dir(A-G)); dot("$B$", B, dir(B-G)); dot("$C$", C, dir(C-G)); dot("$D$", D, dir(-90)); dot("$M$", M, dir(-90)); \end{asy} \end{center} This implies that $BD = 4$ and $AD = 4\sqrt{3}$. In addition, since $AM = BM$ and $\angle ABM = 60^\circ$, it follows that $\triangle ABM$ is equilateral. Thus $BM = AB = 8$, so it follows that $ DM = BM - BD = 4. $ Since $M$ is the midpoint of $CD$, we get that $CM = DM = 4$, so $CD = 8$. Now, we can use the Pythagorean Theorem on right triangle $\triangle ADC$ to get $ AC^2 = (4\sqrt{3})^2 + 8^2 = 64 + 48 = \boxed{112}. $

input: Let $P$ denote the product of all positive integers $n$ such that the least common multiple of $2n$ and $n^2$ is $62n-336$. Compute the remainder when $P$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: Note that when $n$ is even, $\opname{lcm} (2n,n^2) = n^2$, but when $n$ is odd, $\opname{lcm} (2n,n^2) = 2n^2$. We consider these two cases separately. \begin{itemize} \item If $n$ is even, then $\opname{lcm}(2n,n^2) = n^2 = 62n -336$. We can rearrange this to get $ n^2 - 62n + 336 = 0 $ which factors as $ (n-6)(n-56) = 0 $ so $n = 6$ and $n = 56$; and these both work because they are even. \item If $n$ is odd, then $\opname{lcm}(2n,n^2) = 2n^2 = 62n - 336$. This implies that $ 2n^2 - 62n + 336 = 0 \implies n^2 - 31n + 168 = 0. $ Factor to get $ (n-7)(n-24) = 0. $ However, $n=24$ is even, so the only solution in this case is $n = 7$, which works. \end{itemize} Multiplying all of the solutions gives $6\cdot 7 \cdot 56$, which is $2352$. Thus, $\boxed{352}$ is our answer.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 352, which is correct.

input: Let $x$ be the unique positive real number satisfying $ 2^x+32^x=8^x+16^x. $ Compute $8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}$.

metadata.solution: Let $y = 2^x$. Note that our desired extraction $8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}$ can be written as $64y^3 - 64y = 64(y^3 - y)$. We can then write our given equation as $ y + y^5 = y^3 + y^4. $ Note that $y = 0, 1$ satisfy this equation so this factors as $ y(y-1)(y^3 - y - 1) = 0. $ Since $x > 0$, it follows that $y > 1$ and thus $y^3 - y = 1$. As such, the answer is $64(y^3 - y) = 64 \cdot 1 = \boxed{64}$ as desired.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 64, which is correct.

Since $x > 0$, we have $a > 1$. The equation becomes: $$1 + a^4 = a^2 + a^3$$ $$a^4 - a^3 - a^2 + 1 = 0$$ **Step 4: Factor the polynomial** Factor the quartic equation: $$a^4 - a^3 - a^2 + 1 = (a - 1)(a^3 - a - 1) = 0$$ Since $a > 1$, $a \neq 1$. input: Compute the number of integers less than $1000$ which can be written in the form $ x^{\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor} + \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor^x $ for some positive real number $x$.

metadata.solution: Let $S = x^{\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor} + \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor^x$. We will use casework on $n \coloneqq \lfloor x\rfloor \ge 0$. In the edge case $n = 0$ (and $x \neq 0$), the sum has fixed value $S = 0^x + x^0 = 1$, which gives $1$ value. Now for a fixed choice of $n \ge 1$, the value of $S$ is strictly monotonically increasing in the interval $n \le x < n+1$. Hence the value of $S$ will range across $ 2n^n \le S < (n+1)^n + n^{n+1} $ for $n > 0$. In particular, if $n \ge 5$ then $S > 1000$. For $1 \le n \le 4$, we see the values of $S$ that appear are \begin{align*} n = 1 &\implies 2 \le S < 3 \\ n = 2 &\implies 8 \le S < 17 \\ n = 3 &\implies 54 \le S < 145 \\ n = 4 &\implies 512 \le S < 1649. \end{align*} Hence the total count is $1 + 1 + 9 + 91 + 488 = \boxed{590}$.

input: Rosa the otter is stacking $53$ blocks in a tower. For $n \ge 1$, after successfully placing the previous $n-1$ blocks, the probability that placing the $n$\ts{th} block causes the whole tower to topple is $\frac{1}{54 - n}$. Compute the expected number of blocks placed successfully before the block that causes the tower to topple.

metadata.solution: Note that the tower must collapse after successfully placing $n$ blocks for some $1 \le n \le 53$ (as the last block guarantees a collapse). So the probability of the tower collapsing on placing the $n$\ts{th} block is $ \left(1 - \frac{1}{53}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{52}\right) \cdot \dotsm \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{55 - n} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{54 - n} = \frac{52}{53} \cdot \frac{51}{52} \dotsm \frac{54-n}{55-n} \cdot \frac{1}{54-n} = \frac{1}{53}. $ As such, each $1 \le n \le 53$ is equally likely. Thus, the expected number of blocks is thus $ \frac{0}{53} + \frac{1}{53} + \dots + \frac{52}{53} = \frac{52 \cdot 53}{2 \cdot 53} = \boxed{26}. $

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 26, which is correct.

Compute the smallest integer $c$ such that the following statement is true: for every integer $n \ge 2$, given students $A_1$, \dots, $A_n$ satisfying $A_1 \succ A_2 \succ \dots \succ A_n$, the total score of $A_n$ is always at most $c$ points more than the total score of $A_1$.

metadata.solution: The answer is $600 - 2(1+2+3+4+5) = \boxed{570}$. We prove this by providing a construction and then showing it's best possible. \paragraph{Construction} The example with seven students \begin{align*} (5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0) &\succ (4, 3, 2, 1, 0, 100) \succ (3, 2, 1, 0, 100, 99) \\ &\succ (2, 1, 0, 100, 99, 98) \succ (1, 0, 100, 99, 98, 97) \\ &\succ (0, 100, 99, 98, 97, 96) \succ (100, 99, 98, 97, 96, 95) \end{align*} is a construction for $c = 570$, since the first student has total score $5+4+3+2+1=15$ and the last student has total score $100+99+98+97+96+95=585$. \paragraph{Bound} We now show that no higher value can be achieved. The two main claims are that if the score of $A_1$ is too low, or the score of $A_n$ is too high, then $n \le 5$. We state and prove these claims in turn: \begin{claim*} If the total score of $A_1$ is less than $15$, then there cannot exist students $A_2$, $A_3$, $A_4$, $A_5$, $A_6$ such that $ A_1 \succ A_2 \succ A_3 \succ A_4 \succ \dots \succ A_6. $ In other words, we have $n...

input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

metadata.solution: We consider two disjoint cases. \begin{itemize} \ii If $x$ is an integer, then it obviously works. Thus, we get $100$ solutions for $x \in \{1,2,\dots,100\}$. \ii Now suppose $x$ is not an integer, so we may set $n = \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor$ and $n+1 = \left\lceil x \right\rceil$. Then, the equation reads $ x^2 = n(n+1) \implies x = \sqrt{n(n+1)}. $ In other words, for each integer $n$, there is \emph{at most} one non-integer value of $x$ fulfilling the equation in the range $n < x < n+1$, namely $x = \sqrt{n(n+1)}$. On the other hand, the inequality $n < \sqrt{n(n+1)} < n+1$ is true for all $n \geq 1$. So each of the intervals $(1,2)$, $(2,3)$, \dots, $(99,100)$ does indeed have exactly one valid solution; namely, $x = \sqrt{n(n+1)}$ for the $n$th interval. \end{itemize} In total, we obtain $100 + 99 = \boxed{199}$ solutions.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 199, which is correct.

model_graded.answer: Let \[ n=\lfloor x\rfloor ,\qquad m=\lceil x\rceil . \] If \(x\) is an integer then \(n=m=x\) and \[ x^{2}=n\cdot m=x^{2} \] holds for every positive integer \(x\). Hence all integers \(1,2,\dots ,100\) are solutions – 100 of them. If \(x\) is not an integer then \(\lceil x\rceil =\lfloor x\rfloor +1=n+1\) and the equation becomes \[ x^{2}=n(n+1)\qquad (n\ge 0). \] Since \(x>0\), \[ x=\sqrt{n(n+1)} . \] We need \(\sqrt{n(n+1)}\) to lie in the interval \((n,n+1)\) and satisfy \(x\le 100\). For any integer \(n\ge 1\), \[ n<\sqrt{n(n+1)}<n+1, \] so the interval condition holds. The condition \(x\le 100\) gives \[ \sqrt{n(n+1)}\le 100\;\Longleftrightarrow\; n(n+1)\le 10\,000. \] \(n(n+1)\le 10\,000\) holds for \(n=1,2,\dots ,99\) (since \(99\cdot100=9900\le10\,000\) and \(100\cdot101>10\,000\)). Thus each \(n=1,\dots ,99\) yields a distinct non‑integer solution \[ x=\sqrt{n(n+1)} . \] These 99 numbers are not integers because \(n(n+1)\) is never a perfect square for \(n\ge1\) (the only consecutive perfect...

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: Given a $7$-tuple satisfying the given condition, let $S$ be the set of all of integers appearing at least once. Then the condition is equivalent to the tuple satisfying $a_i=i$ for all $i \in S$, because this means that $a_{a_i} = i$, so $a_{a_i} = a_i$. Now, we apply casework on $k = |S|$. There are $\binom{7}{k}$ ways to choose a subset of $S$ with fixed cardinality $k$, and there are $k^{7-k}$ ways to choose $a_j$ for $j \notin S$. Thus, \begin{align*} N &= \sum_{k=1}^{7} \binom{7}{k} k^{7-k} \\ &= 7 \cdot 1 + 21 \cdot 32 + 35 \cdot 81 + 35 \cdot 64 + 21 \cdot 25 + 7 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 1 \\ &= 7 + 672 + 2835 + 2240 + 525 + 42 + 1 = 6322. \end{align*} This gives the answer $\boxed{322}$.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 322, which is correct.

Structure of an idempotent function If \(j\) belongs to the image of \(f\), then \(j=f(i)\) for some \(i\). Applying the idempotent property, \[ f(j)=f(f(i))=f(i)=j . \] Hence every element of the image is a **fixed point** of \(f\). Conversely, if \(j\) is a fixed point then \(j=f(j)\), so \(j\) lies in the image. Therefore \[ \operatorname{Im}(f)=\{x\mid f(x)=x\}. \] In the functional digraph of \(f\) each component is a star: a centre that is a fixed point, and all other vertices point directly to that centre. No longer paths are possible. --- ### 2. Counting the functions Pick the set of fixed points. Let this set have size \(k\) (necessarily \(1\le k\le 7\)). There are \(\binom{7}{k}\) ways to choose it. metadata.solution: \paragraph{Solution by Ritwin Narra.} Note that $c$ divides both $\lcm(b, c)$ and $\lcm(c, a)$, so it follows that $c \mid \gcd(a, b)$ and thus $c$ divides both $a$ and $b$. Conversely, all equations on the curve can be verified to work. \end{proof} Note that $a^3 - a \le b \le a^3 - 1$ in any valid $(a,b,c)$. As the closed intervals $[a^3-a, a^3-1]$ are disjoint across $a \in \ZZ_{>0}$, the cases for different values of $a$ do not overlap. For a fixed $a$, the number of possible values of $b$ is given by $\tau(a)$, the number of positive divisors of $a$. input: Compute the smallest integer $k > 1$ such that there are exactly $10$ even integers $n \ge 2$ for which $k - n/2$ is divisible by $n$.

metadata.solution: Replace $n$ with $2m$ in what follows. Then $k - m$ is divisible by $2m$ when it equals $a \cdot 2m$ for some integer $a$; i.e.\ we require $k = (2a + 1) \cdot m$ for some integer $a$. In other words, $k$ needs to be an odd multiple of $m$. Thus, if $k = 2^e \cdot b$ for some integer $e \ge 0$ and odd integer $b$, then the number of $m$ for which $k$ is an odd multiple is given by the number of divisors of $b$. We thus can assume $e = 0$ and try to minimize $b$; we need to choose the minimal odd integer $b$ with $10$ positive divisors. Recall that the number of factors of a number is the product of one more than the exponents. Since $10 = 2 \cdot 5$, this means that either $k = p^9$ or $k = p^4 \cdot q$ for some primes $p$ and $q$. In the first case $k \ge 3^9$ and in the second case $k \ge 3^4 \cdot 5$; the smaller of these is $3^4 \cdot 5 = \boxed{405}$.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 405, which is correct.

input: There are $2025$ green pencils on a table. Every minute, Elphaba removes two randomly chosen pencils on the table. Right after that, Glinda adds back one pink pencil. After $2023$ minutes, the probability that at least one of the two pencils remaining on the table is green is $\frac{m}{n}$ where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute the remainder when $m+n$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: First, let's consider a particular green pencil $G_1$. The probability that $G_1$ survives all the way to the end is given by \begin{align*} \frac{2023}{2025} \cdot \frac{2022}{2024} \cdot \frac{2021}{2023} \cdot \cdots \cdot \frac{2}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{3} &= \frac{1}{2025} \cdot \frac{1}{2024} \cdot 2 \\ &= \frac{1}{1012 \cdot 2025}. \end{align*} However, note that at the end of the process, the number of green pencils remaining must be either $0$ or $1$. In other words, if $G_2$, \dots, $G_{2025}$ denote the remaining pencils, the event of $G_i$ surviving to the end is disjoint from any other such event. Hence, the probability is just $ \underbrace{ \frac{1}{1012 \cdot 2025} + \dots + \frac{1}{1012 \cdot 2025} }_{2025 \text{times}} = \frac{1}{1012}. $ The remainder when $1+1012$ is divided by $1000$ is $\boxed{013}$.

input: Compute the maximum possible value of $ab+bc+cd+de$ over all choices of positive integers $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, $e$ satisfying $a+b+c+d+e=60$.

metadata.solution: We show three solutions. \paragraph{First solution by inequalities.} Let $S$ denote the desired sum. The idea is to write $ S \coloneqq ab+bc+cd+de = (a+c+e)(b+d) - (ad+be). $ Note that \begin{itemize} \ii We have $ (a+c+e)(b+d) = (a+c+e)(60-(a+c+e)) = 900 - ((a+c+e)-30)^2. $ \ii We have $ad + be \ge (a+d-1) + (b+e-1) = 58 - c$. \end{itemize} Hence, we get an estimate of $ S \le 842 + \left( c - ((a+c+e)-30)^2 \right) . $ Now we consider cases on $c$. \begin{itemize} \ii If $c \le 28$, we get $S \le 842 + c \le 870$. Equality can indeed occur, for example if $a=e=1$, $c=28$, and $b+d=30$. \ii Now assume $c > 28$. Then $a+c+e > 30$, and from $a,e \ge 1$ we can make the estimate $ (a+c+e-30)^2 \ge (c-28)^2. $ Thus, we have $ S \le 842 + \left( c - (c-28)^2 \right). $ For every value of $c > 28$, it is easy to check $ c - (c- 28)^2 \le 28 $ so we have $c \le 870$ in this case too. And equality can indeed occur, for example if $a=e=1$, $c=29$, and $b+d=29$. \end{itemize} Hence we get a maximum of $\boxed{870}$...

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 870, which is correct.

input: Winston forgot the definition of a prime number. He instead defines a \emph{New-prime} recursively as follows: \begin{itemize} \ii $1$ is not New-prime. \ii A positive integer $n > 1$ is New-prime if and only if $n$ cannot be expressed as the product of exactly two (not necessarily distinct) New-prime positive integers. \end{itemize} Compute the number of positive integers dividing $5005^4$ which are New-primes.

metadata.solution: The main claim is: \begin{claim*} A positive integer is New-prime if and only if the sum of the exponents of its prime factors is odd. \end{claim*} \begin{proof} By induction on $n \ge 1$, with the base case given. Consider any $n$. If the sum of the exponents of $n$ is odd, then clearly it is not the product of two numbers whose sum of exponents is odd. Otherwise, if the sum of exponents of $n$ is even, consider any prime $p\mid n$. Then, $p$ and $\frac{n}{p}$ are both New-prime, since $\frac{n}{p}$ has an odd sum of exponents and is less than $n$. The induction applies, so this condition for New-primes works. \end{proof} Hence we need the number of divisors of $5005^4 = 5^4 \cdot 7^4 \cdot 11^4 \cdot 13^4$ with an odd exponent sum. This can be computed by using a roots of unity filter on $ P(X) \coloneqq (1 + X + X^2 + X^3 + X^4)^4 $ and looking for the sum of the coefficients of the terms with odd degree; that is $ \frac{P(1)-P(-1)}{2} = \frac{625-1}{2} = \boxed{312}. $

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 312, which is correct.

model_graded.answer: The “New‑prime’’ numbers are precisely the integers \(n>1\) with an odd number of prime factors (counted with multiplicity). Indeed, if \(\Omega(n)\) denotes the total number of prime factors of \(n\), then * if \(\Omega(n)\) is odd, \(n\) cannot be written as a product of two New‑primes (the sum of two odd \(\Omega\)’s would be even), so \(n\) is New‑prime; * if \(\Omega(n)\) is even, write \(n=p\cdot (n/p)\) where \(p\) is any prime divisor. Both \(p\) and \(n/p\) have odd \(\Omega\), hence are New‑primes, so \(n\) is **not** New‑prime. Thus a divisor of \(5005^{4}\) is a New‑prime iff the sum of the exponents in its prime factorisation is odd. Factorisation: \[ 5005 = 5\cdot7\cdot11\cdot13 \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad 5005^{4}=5^{4}\,7^{4}\,11^{4}\,13^{4}. \] A divisor of \(5005^{4}\) has the form \(5^{a}7^{b}11^{c}13^{d}\) with \(0\le a,b,c,d\le4\). input: There is a unique triplet of integers $(a, b, c)$ such that $0 < a < b < c < 1000$ and $ \frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c=\frac{1}{315}. $ Compute $a$.

metadata.solution: The key observation is that $ \half \cdot \frac{1}{315} = \frac{3}{42 \cdot 45} = \frac{1}{42} - \frac{1}{45}. $ Then one can write the right-hand side as the sum of three Egyptian fractions as follows: \begin{align*} \half \cdot \frac{1}{315} = \frac{1}{42} - \frac{1}{45} &= \left( \frac{1}{42} - \frac{1}{43} \right) + \left( \frac{1}{43} - \frac{1}{44} \right) + \left( \frac{1}{44} - \frac{1}{45} \right) \\ &= \frac{1}{42 \cdot 43} + \frac{1}{43 \cdot 44} + \frac{1}{44 \cdot 45}. \end{align*} Doubling everything gives $ \frac{1}{315} = \frac{2}{42 \cdot 43} + \frac{2}{43 \cdot 44} + \frac{2}{44 \cdot 45}. $ Hence one can take $a = \frac{42 \cdot 43}{2}$, $b = \frac{43 \cdot 44}{2}$, $c = \frac{44 \cdot 45}{2}$. So $a = \boxed{903}$.

input: Alice has a deck of $2000$ cards, numbered $1$ through $2000$. Alice chooses an integer $1 \le n < 1000$ and deals Cheshire a random subset of $2n-1$ of the cards without repetition. metadata.solution: Fix a positive integer $n < 1000$. A subset of $2n - 1$ cards is called \emph{good} if it contains a collection of $n$ consecutively numbered cards. To enumerate the good subsets, we categorize them into buckets $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_{2000}$. Let $B_k$ be the set of all subsets $S$ of $2n - 1$ cards such that: $ k-1 \notin S \quad \text{and} \quad k, k+1, \dots, k+n-1 \in S. $ Note that every element of $B_k$ is a good subset, and every good subset is an element of some bucket $B_k$. Furthermore, every subset is part of at most $1$ bucket because a subset of $2n - 1$ cards cannot contain $2$ disjoint collections of $n$ consecutively numbered cards. Therefore the probability of Cheshire winning is given by: $ P_n = \frac {\lvert B_1 \rvert + \lvert B_2 \rvert + \dots + \lvert B_{2000} \rvert} {\binom{2000}{2n - 1}} $ By the definitions of $B_k$ we also have that: $ B_k = \begin{cases} \binom{2000 - n}{n - 1} & \text{if $k = 1$} \\ \binom{2000 - n - 1}{n - 1} & \text{if $2 \leq k \leq 2000-n$} \\ 0 & \text{if...

input: Let $P$ denote the product of all positive integers $n$ such that the least common multiple of $2n$ and $n^2$ is $62n-336$. Compute the remainder when $P$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: Note that when $n$ is even, $\opname{lcm} (2n,n^2) = n^2$, but when $n$ is odd, $\opname{lcm} (2n,n^2) = 2n^2$. We consider these two cases separately. \begin{itemize} \item If $n$ is even, then $\opname{lcm}(2n,n^2) = n^2 = 62n -336$. We can rearrange this to get $ n^2 - 62n + 336 = 0 $ which factors as $ (n-6)(n-56) = 0 $ so $n = 6$ and $n = 56$; and these both work because they are even. \item If $n$ is odd, then $\opname{lcm}(2n,n^2) = 2n^2 = 62n - 336$. This implies that $ 2n^2 - 62n + 336 = 0 \implies n^2 - 31n + 168 = 0. $ Factor to get $ (n-7)(n-24) = 0. $ However, $n=24$ is even, so the only solution in this case is $n = 7$, which works. \end{itemize} Multiplying all of the solutions gives $6\cdot 7 \cdot 56$, which is $2352$. Thus, $\boxed{352}$ is our answer.

input: Let $x$ be the unique positive real number satisfying $ 2^x+32^x=8^x+16^x. $ Compute $8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}$.

metadata.solution: Let $y = 2^x$. Note that our desired extraction $8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}$ can be written as $64y^3 - 64y = 64(y^3 - y)$. We can then write our given equation as $ y + y^5 = y^3 + y^4. $ Note that $y = 0, 1$ satisfy this equation so this factors as $ y(y-1)(y^3 - y - 1) = 0. $ Since $x > 0$, it follows that $y > 1$ and thus $y^3 - y = 1$. As such, the answer is $64(y^3 - y) = 64 \cdot 1 = \boxed{64}$ as desired.

input: Compute the number of integers less than $1000$ which can be written in the form $ x^{\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor} + \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor^x $ for some positive real number $x$.

metadata.solution: Let $S = x^{\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor} + \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor^x$. We will use casework on $n \coloneqq \lfloor x\rfloor \ge 0$. In the edge case $n = 0$ (and $x \neq 0$), the sum has fixed value $S = 0^x + x^0 = 1$, which gives $1$ value. Now for a fixed choice of $n \ge 1$, the value of $S$ is strictly monotonically increasing in the interval $n \le x < n+1$. Hence the value of $S$ will range across $ 2n^n \le S < (n+1)^n + n^{n+1} $ for $n > 0$. In particular, if $n \ge 5$ then $S > 1000$. For $1 \le n \le 4$, we see the values of $S$ that appear are \begin{align*} n = 1 &\implies 2 \le S < 3 \\ n = 2 &\implies 8 \le S < 17 \\ n = 3 &\implies 54 \le S < 145 \\ n = 4 &\implies 512 \le S < 1649. \end{align*} Hence the total count is $1 + 1 + 9 + 91 + 488 = \boxed{590}$.

input: Rosa the otter is stacking $53$ blocks in a tower. For $n \ge 1$, after successfully placing the previous $n-1$ blocks, the probability that placing the $n$\ts{th} block causes the whole tower to topple is $\frac{1}{54 - n}$. Compute the expected number of blocks placed successfully before the block that causes the tower to topple.

metadata.solution: Note that the tower must collapse after successfully placing $n$ blocks for some $1 \le n \le 53$ (as the last block guarantees a collapse). So the probability of the tower collapsing on placing the $n$\ts{th} block is $ \left(1 - \frac{1}{53}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{52}\right) \cdot \dotsm \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{55 - n} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{54 - n} = \frac{52}{53} \cdot \frac{51}{52} \dotsm \frac{54-n}{55-n} \cdot \frac{1}{54-n} = \frac{1}{53}. $ As such, each $1 \le n \le 53$ is equally likely. Thus, the expected number of blocks is thus $ \frac{0}{53} + \frac{1}{53} + \dots + \frac{52}{53} = \frac{52 \cdot 53}{2 \cdot 53} = \boxed{26}. $

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 26, which is correct.

model_graded.answer: # 终末期肾病患者心理痛苦体验质性研究的Meta整合讨论报告 **专家审阅版·循证护理深度分析** --- ## 摘要 本报告基于10项已完成的质性研究整合（涵盖青年等待移植、围透析期、维持性血液透析及肾移植后等不同阶段患者），从方法学严谨性、主题异质性、理论深度及临床适用性四个维度展开批判性比较。通过引入2021-2025年间发表的23篇权威新证据（包括8篇SCI/SSCI顶级期刊、12篇中国科学引文数据库核心库及北大核心期刊论文、3部国际临床指南与理论框架），系统阐释了ESRD患者心理痛苦的动态演化机制、文化特异性表现及未被充分关注的照护盲区。报告创新性地提出"三维痛苦累积模型"（生理-存在-关系维度），并针对当前整合研究的局限，从精准护理干预、数字健康技术应用及家庭系统赋权三方面提出未来研究方向。所有证据均采用JBI质性研究评价标准严格分级，确保护理决策的循证强度。 --- ## 1. 纳入研究的方法学质量与主题饱和度对比分析 ### 1.1 研究设计与方法学严谨性评估 本整合所纳入的10项研究在哲学范式与方法论选择上呈现显著异质性。刘典等[1]采用解释性现象学方法（IPA），通过对15例青年患者（18-35岁）的深度访谈，揭示了等待肾移植期间"时间悬置"的独特痛苦维度，其样本量虽小但数据饱和度达92%，符合IPA对同质性样本的严苛要求。何雪松[2]运用扎根理论，从50例患者的疾痛叙事中提炼出"过坎"理论模型，其理论抽样策略与持续比较法应用规范，但缺乏研究者反身性（reflexivity）报告，可能引入主观偏倚[11]。杨桃先等[3]与伍小燕[4]均采用描述性质性研究，前者聚焦围透析期（n=20）的"决策困境"，后者探索维持性血液透析（MHD）患者的"慢性哀伤"，两者在伦理审查细节与访谈时长记录上存在报告不完整问题，影响可转移性评价[12]。 国际研究方面，Frontini等[6]采用多国多中心横断面质性设计（n=42对医患二元组），运用主题分析法（Thematic...

input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

metadata.solution: We consider two disjoint cases. \begin{itemize} \ii If $x$ is an integer, then it obviously works. Thus, we get $100$ solutions for $x \in \{1,2,\dots,100\}$. \ii Now suppose $x$ is not an integer, so we may set $n = \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor$ and $n+1 = \left\lceil x \right\rceil$. Then, the equation reads $ x^2 = n(n+1) \implies x = \sqrt{n(n+1)}. $ In other words, for each integer $n$, there is \emph{at most} one non-integer value of $x$ fulfilling the equation in the range $n < x < n+1$, namely $x = \sqrt{n(n+1)}$. On the other hand, the inequality $n < \sqrt{n(n+1)} < n+1$ is true for all $n \geq 1$. So each of the intervals $(1,2)$, $(2,3)$, \dots, $(99,100)$ does indeed have exactly one valid solution; namely, $x = \sqrt{n(n+1)}$ for the $n$th interval. \end{itemize} In total, we obtain $100 + 99 = \boxed{199}$ solutions.

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: Given a $7$-tuple satisfying the given condition, let $S$ be the set of all of integers appearing at least once. Then the condition is equivalent to the tuple satisfying $a_i=i$ for all $i \in S$, because this means that $a_{a_i} = i$, so $a_{a_i} = a_i$. Now, we apply casework on $k = |S|$. There are $\binom{7}{k}$ ways to choose a subset of $S$ with fixed cardinality $k$, and there are $k^{7-k}$ ways to choose $a_j$ for $j \notin S$. Thus, \begin{align*} N &= \sum_{k=1}^{7} \binom{7}{k} k^{7-k} \\ &= 7 \cdot 1 + 21 \cdot 32 + 35 \cdot 81 + 35 \cdot 64 + 21 \cdot 25 + 7 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 1 \\ &= 7 + 672 + 2835 + 2240 + 525 + 42 + 1 = 6322. \end{align*} This gives the answer $\boxed{322}$.

metadata.solution: \paragraph{First solution using complex numbers.} We first consider the analogous problem where $\omega$ has radius $1$ instead of $12$ (and will multiply the answer by $144$ to account for scaling at the end). To see why this is true, observe that if we fix $b$, $\frac{a}{b}$ is just $a$ shifted counterclockwise by an angle of $\arg b$, so it still reflects a uniform point on the unit circle. Every peg of \(\mathcal G_n\) has even degree: * the three corners have degree \(2\); * the pegs on the sides (but not the corners) have degree \(4\); * the interior pegs have degree \(6\). We start by identifying the equation of the parabola. \begin{claim*} The parabola is the zero locus of $ P(x, y) = 9x^2 - 18x + 9 - 6y + y^2 - 6xy. $ \end{claim*} \begin{proof} Since $P$ is a conic, its equation should take the form $ P(x,y) = Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0. $ Our goal is determine all the constants $A$, $B$, \dots, $F$; these constants will be unique up to scaling. Since the parabola is tangent to the $x$-axis at $x=1$, we have that $P(x, 0)$ is of the form $k(x-1)^2$ for some $k$. Similarly, $P(0,y)$ is of the form $\ell(y-3)^2$ for some $\ell$. input: Compute the smallest integer $k > 1$ such that there are exactly $10$ even integers $n \ge 2$ for which $k - n/2$ is divisible by $n$.

metadata.solution: Replace $n$ with $2m$ in what follows. Then $k - m$ is divisible by $2m$ when it equals $a \cdot 2m$ for some integer $a$; i.e.\ we require $k = (2a + 1) \cdot m$ for some integer $a$. In other words, $k$ needs to be an odd multiple of $m$. Thus, if $k = 2^e \cdot b$ for some integer $e \ge 0$ and odd integer $b$, then the number of $m$ for which $k$ is an odd multiple is given by the number of divisors of $b$. We thus can assume $e = 0$ and try to minimize $b$; we need to choose the minimal odd integer $b$ with $10$ positive divisors. Recall that the number of factors of a number is the product of one more than the exponents. Since $10 = 2 \cdot 5$, this means that either $k = p^9$ or $k = p^4 \cdot q$ for some primes $p$ and $q$. In the first case $k \ge 3^9$ and in the second case $k \ge 3^4 \cdot 5$; the smaller of these is $3^4 \cdot 5 = \boxed{405}$.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 405, which is correct.

input: There are $2025$ green pencils on a table. Every minute, Elphaba removes two randomly chosen pencils on the table. Right after that, Glinda adds back one pink pencil. After $2023$ minutes, the probability that at least one of the two pencils remaining on the table is green is $\frac{m}{n}$ where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute the remainder when $m+n$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: First, let's consider a particular green pencil $G_1$. The probability that $G_1$ survives all the way to the end is given by \begin{align*} \frac{2023}{2025} \cdot \frac{2022}{2024} \cdot \frac{2021}{2023} \cdot \cdots \cdot \frac{2}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{3} &= \frac{1}{2025} \cdot \frac{1}{2024} \cdot 2 \\ &= \frac{1}{1012 \cdot 2025}. \end{align*} However, note that at the end of the process, the number of green pencils remaining must be either $0$ or $1$. In other words, if $G_2$, \dots, $G_{2025}$ denote the remaining pencils, the event of $G_i$ surviving to the end is disjoint from any other such event. Hence, the probability is just $ \underbrace{ \frac{1}{1012 \cdot 2025} + \dots + \frac{1}{1012 \cdot 2025} }_{2025 \text{times}} = \frac{1}{1012}. $ The remainder when $1+1012$ is divided by $1000$ is $\boxed{013}$.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 13, which is correct.

In each minute two pencils are chosen uniformly at random and removed; then a new pink pencil is added. After \(N-2=2023\) minutes only two pencils remain. input: Compute the maximum possible value of $ab+bc+cd+de$ over all choices of positive integers $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, $e$ satisfying $a+b+c+d+e=60$.

metadata.solution: We show three solutions. \paragraph{First solution by inequalities.} Let $S$ denote the desired sum. The idea is to write $ S \coloneqq ab+bc+cd+de = (a+c+e)(b+d) - (ad+be). $ Note that \begin{itemize} \ii We have $ (a+c+e)(b+d) = (a+c+e)(60-(a+c+e)) = 900 - ((a+c+e)-30)^2. $ \ii We have $ad + be \ge (a+d-1) + (b+e-1) = 58 - c$. \end{itemize} Hence, we get an estimate of $ S \le 842 + \left( c - ((a+c+e)-30)^2 \right) . $ Now we consider cases on $c$. \begin{itemize} \ii If $c \le 28$, we get $S \le 842 + c \le 870$. Equality can indeed occur, for example if $a=e=1$, $c=28$, and $b+d=30$. \ii Now assume $c > 28$. Then $a+c+e > 30$, and from $a,e \ge 1$ we can make the estimate $ (a+c+e-30)^2 \ge (c-28)^2. $ Thus, we have $ S \le 842 + \left( c - (c-28)^2 \right). $ For every value of $c > 28$, it is easy to check $ c - (c- 28)^2 \le 28 $ so we have $c \le 870$ in this case too. And equality can indeed occur, for example if $a=e=1$, $c=29$, and $b+d=29$. \end{itemize} Hence we get a maximum of $\boxed{870}$...

input: Winston forgot the definition of a prime number. He instead defines a \emph{New-prime} recursively as follows: \begin{itemize} \ii $1$ is not New-prime. \ii A positive integer $n > 1$ is New-prime if and only if $n$ cannot be expressed as the product of exactly two (not necessarily distinct) New-prime positive integers. \end{itemize} Compute the number of positive integers dividing $5005^4$ which are New-primes.

metadata.solution: The main claim is: \begin{claim*} A positive integer is New-prime if and only if the sum of the exponents of its prime factors is odd. \end{claim*} \begin{proof} By induction on $n \ge 1$, with the base case given. Consider any $n$. If the sum of the exponents of $n$ is odd, then clearly it is not the product of two numbers whose sum of exponents is odd. Otherwise, if the sum of exponents of $n$ is even, consider any prime $p\mid n$. Then, $p$ and $\frac{n}{p}$ are both New-prime, since $\frac{n}{p}$ has an odd sum of exponents and is less than $n$. The induction applies, so this condition for New-primes works. \end{proof} Hence we need the number of divisors of $5005^4 = 5^4 \cdot 7^4 \cdot 11^4 \cdot 13^4$ with an odd exponent sum. This can be computed by using a roots of unity filter on $ P(X) \coloneqq (1 + X + X^2 + X^3 + X^4)^4 $ and looking for the sum of the coefficients of the terms with odd degree; that is $ \frac{P(1)-P(-1)}{2} = \frac{625-1}{2} = \boxed{312}. $

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 312, which is correct.

Characterising New‑primes Write \(\Omega(n)\) for the number of prime factors of \(n\) (with multiplicity). input: There is a unique triplet of integers $(a, b, c)$ such that $0 < a < b < c < 1000$ and $ \frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c=\frac{1}{315}. $ Compute $a$.

metadata.solution: The key observation is that $ \half \cdot \frac{1}{315} = \frac{3}{42 \cdot 45} = \frac{1}{42} - \frac{1}{45}. $ Then one can write the right-hand side as the sum of three Egyptian fractions as follows: \begin{align*} \half \cdot \frac{1}{315} = \frac{1}{42} - \frac{1}{45} &= \left( \frac{1}{42} - \frac{1}{43} \right) + \left( \frac{1}{43} - \frac{1}{44} \right) + \left( \frac{1}{44} - \frac{1}{45} \right) \\ &= \frac{1}{42 \cdot 43} + \frac{1}{43 \cdot 44} + \frac{1}{44 \cdot 45}. \end{align*} Doubling everything gives $ \frac{1}{315} = \frac{2}{42 \cdot 43} + \frac{2}{43 \cdot 44} + \frac{2}{44 \cdot 45}. $ Hence one can take $a = \frac{42 \cdot 43}{2}$, $b = \frac{43 \cdot 44}{2}$, $c = \frac{44 \cdot 45}{2}$. So $a = \boxed{903}$.

input: Alice has a deck of $2000$ cards, numbered $1$ through $2000$. Alice chooses an integer $1 \le n < 1000$ and deals Cheshire a random subset of $2n-1$ of the cards without repetition. Cheshire wins if the cards dealt contain any $n$ consecutively numbered cards. Compute the value of $n$ Alice should choose to minimize Cheshire's chances of winning.

metadata.solution: Fix a positive integer $n < 1000$. A subset of $2n - 1$ cards is called \emph{good} if it contains a collection of $n$ consecutively numbered cards. To enumerate the good subsets, we categorize them into buckets $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_{2000}$. Let $B_k$ be the set of all subsets $S$ of $2n - 1$ cards such that: $ k-1 \notin S \quad \text{and} \quad k, k+1, \dots, k+n-1 \in S. $ Note that every element of $B_k$ is a good subset, and every good subset is an element of some bucket $B_k$. Furthermore, every subset is part of at most $1$ bucket because a subset of $2n - 1$ cards cannot contain $2$ disjoint collections of $n$ consecutively numbered cards. Therefore the probability of Cheshire winning is given by: $ P_n = \frac {\lvert B_1 \rvert + \lvert B_2 \rvert + \dots + \lvert B_{2000} \rvert} {\binom{2000}{2n - 1}} $ By the definitions of $B_k$ we also have that: $ B_k = \begin{cases} \binom{2000 - n}{n - 1} & \text{if $k = 1$} \\ \binom{2000 - n - 1}{n - 1} & \text{if $2 \leq k \leq 2000-n$} \\ 0 & \text{if...

input: Let $ABC$ be a triangle with $\angle B = 60\dg$ and $AB = 8$. Let $D$ be the foot of the altitude from $A$ to $BC$, and let $M$ be the midpoint of $CD$. If $AM = BM$, compute $AC^2$.

metadata.solution: Since $\angle ABD = 60\dg$ and $\angle ADB = 90\dg$, it follows that $\triangle ABD$ is a $30\dg$-$60\dg$-$90\dg$ triangle. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); pair B = (0, 0); pair A = (4, 4sqrt(3)); pair C = (12, 0); pair D = (4, 0); pair M = (8, 0); pair G = (A + B + C)/3; filldraw(A--B--C--cycle, opacity(0.4)+palered, darkred+1); draw(A--M, darkred+1); draw(A--D, black); label("$8$", midpoint(A--B), dir(150)); dot("$A$", A, dir(A-G)); dot("$B$", B, dir(B-G)); dot("$C$", C, dir(C-G)); dot("$D$", D, dir(-90)); dot("$M$", M, dir(-90)); \end{asy} \end{center} This implies that $BD = 4$ and $AD = 4\sqrt{3}$. In addition, since $AM = BM$ and $\angle ABM = 60^\circ$, it follows that $\triangle ABM$ is equilateral. Thus $BM = AB = 8$, so it follows that $ DM = BM - BD = 4. $ Since $M$ is the midpoint of $CD$, we get that $CM = DM = 4$, so $CD = 8$. Now, we can use the Pythagorean Theorem on right triangle $\triangle ADC$ to get $ AC^2 = (4\sqrt{3})^2 + 8^2 = 64 + 48 = \boxed{112}. $

input: Let $P$ denote the product of all positive integers $n$ such that the least common multiple of $2n$ and $n^2$ is $62n-336$. Compute the remainder when $P$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: Note that when $n$ is even, $\opname{lcm} (2n,n^2) = n^2$, but when $n$ is odd, $\opname{lcm} (2n,n^2) = 2n^2$. We consider these two cases separately. \begin{itemize} \item If $n$ is even, then $\opname{lcm}(2n,n^2) = n^2 = 62n -336$. We can rearrange this to get $ n^2 - 62n + 336 = 0 $ which factors as $ (n-6)(n-56) = 0 $ so $n = 6$ and $n = 56$; and these both work because they are even. \item If $n$ is odd, then $\opname{lcm}(2n,n^2) = 2n^2 = 62n - 336$. This implies that $ 2n^2 - 62n + 336 = 0 \implies n^2 - 31n + 168 = 0. $ Factor to get $ (n-7)(n-24) = 0. $ However, $n=24$ is even, so the only solution in this case is $n = 7$, which works. \end{itemize} Multiplying all of the solutions gives $6\cdot 7 \cdot 56$, which is $2352$. Thus, $\boxed{352}$ is our answer.

input: Let $x$ be the unique positive real number satisfying $ 2^x+32^x=8^x+16^x. $ Compute $8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}$.

metadata.solution: Let $y = 2^x$. Note that our desired extraction $8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}$ can be written as $64y^3 - 64y = 64(y^3 - y)$. We can then write our given equation as $ y + y^5 = y^3 + y^4. $ Note that $y = 0, 1$ satisfy this equation so this factors as $ y(y-1)(y^3 - y - 1) = 0. $ Since $x > 0$, it follows that $y > 1$ and thus $y^3 - y = 1$. As such, the answer is $64(y^3 - y) = 64 \cdot 1 = \boxed{64}$ as desired.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 64, which is correct.

model_graded.answer: Let \[ 2^x+32^x=8^x+16^x . \] Write each term as a power of \(2\): \[ 2^x+(2^5)^x=(2^3)^x+(2^4)^x \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad 2^x+2^{5x}=2^{3x}+2^{4x}. \] Set \[ t=2^x>0 . \] Then \[ t+t^5=t^3+t^4 \;\Longrightarrow\; t^5-t^4-t^3+t=0 \;\Longrightarrow\; t\bigl(t^4-t^3-t^2+1\bigr)=0 . \] Since \(t>0\), \[ t^4-t^3-t^2+1=0. \] Factor: \[ t^4-t^3-t^2+1=(t-1)(t^3-t-1)=0 . \] \(t=1\) gives \(x=0\), which is not positive. The required positive solution satisfies \[ t^3-t-1=0\qquad (t>0). \] Hence \[ t^3-t=1. \tag{1} \] Now compute the desired expression. \[ \begin{aligned} 8^{x+2}&=(2^3)^{x+2}=2^{3x+6}=2^{6}\,(2^x)^3=64\,t^3,\\[2pt] 2^{x+6}&=2^6\cdot 2^x=64\,t. \end{aligned} \] Therefore \[ 8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}=64\,t^3-64\,t=64\,(t^3-t). \] Using (1), \(t^3-t=1\), so \[ 8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}=64\cdot 1=64. \] Thus the answer is \(\boxed{064}\).

input: Compute the number of integers less than $1000$ which can be written in the form $ x^{\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor} + \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor^x $ for some positive real number $x$.

metadata.solution: Let $S = x^{\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor} + \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor^x$. We will use casework on $n \coloneqq \lfloor x\rfloor \ge 0$. In the edge case $n = 0$ (and $x \neq 0$), the sum has fixed value $S = 0^x + x^0 = 1$, which gives $1$ value. Now for a fixed choice of $n \ge 1$, the value of $S$ is strictly monotonically increasing in the interval $n \le x < n+1$. Hence the value of $S$ will range across $ 2n^n \le S < (n+1)^n + n^{n+1} $ for $n > 0$. In particular, if $n \ge 5$ then $S > 1000$. For $1 \le n \le 4$, we see the values of $S$ that appear are \begin{align*} n = 1 &\implies 2 \le S < 3 \\ n = 2 &\implies 8 \le S < 17 \\ n = 3 &\implies 54 \le S < 145 \\ n = 4 &\implies 512 \le S < 1649. \end{align*} Hence the total count is $1 + 1 + 9 + 91 + 488 = \boxed{590}$.

input: Rosa the otter is stacking $53$ blocks in a tower. For $n \ge 1$, after successfully placing the previous $n-1$ blocks, the probability that placing the $n$\ts{th} block causes the whole tower to topple is $\frac{1}{54 - n}$. Compute the expected number of blocks placed successfully before the block that causes the tower to topple.

metadata.solution: Note that the tower must collapse after successfully placing $n$ blocks for some $1 \le n \le 53$ (as the last block guarantees a collapse). So the probability of the tower collapsing on placing the $n$\ts{th} block is $ \left(1 - \frac{1}{53}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{52}\right) \cdot \dotsm \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{55 - n} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{54 - n} = \frac{52}{53} \cdot \frac{51}{52} \dotsm \frac{54-n}{55-n} \cdot \frac{1}{54-n} = \frac{1}{53}. $ As such, each $1 \le n \le 53$ is equally likely. Thus, the expected number of blocks is thus $ \frac{0}{53} + \frac{1}{53} + \dots + \frac{52}{53} = \frac{52 \cdot 53}{2 \cdot 53} = \boxed{26}. $

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 26, which is correct.

model_graded.answer: # 终末期肾病患者心理痛苦体验的Meta整合研究：文献对比与循证讨论 **报告撰写说明**：本报告基于护理研究生循证作业需求，系统对比已纳入Meta整合的12篇文献（实际列出10篇），并严格按照2021-2025年发表、科核/北核/SCI标准补充讨论所需新证据。全文采用质性研究Meta整合的JBI框架与循证护理实践模式，从多角度构建学术论证。 --- ## 一、纳入文献的系统对比分析 ### （一）研究设计与方法学质量评估 您纳入的12篇文献（实际清单为10篇，编号[1-10]）在方法学上呈现"中西融合、质性主导"的特征。中文研究普遍采用现象学或扎根理论传统：**刘典等[1]**、**何雪松[2]**、**杨桃先等[3]**、**伍小燕[4]**、**何宇彬等[5]**均使用半结构深度访谈，样本量12-20例，符合质性研究饱和原则。英文文献中，**Frontini等[6]**采用解释性现象学分析（IPA），创新性地纳入患者-家属二元组访谈；**Cai等[7]**运用建构主义扎根理论，揭示习得性无助的触发机制；**McKeaveney等[8]**采用案例研究法追踪肾移植后患者体验；**Wen等[9]**与**Wube等[10]**均为描述性质性研究。 **方法学局限性对比**：中文研究[1-5]在哲学立场阐述、研究者反身性（reflexivity）报告方面较为薄弱，仅何雪松[2]明确提及研究者自身透析科工作经验对数据诠释的影响。而英文研究[6-10]普遍遵循COREQ或SRQR报告规范，如**Cai等[7]**详细描述了研究者团队组成、预访谈培训及双人背对背编码过程，方法学严谨性显著优于中文文献。 --- ### （二）研究对象特征与情境特异性对比 | 文献 | 研究对象 | 关键情境 | 核心概念 | |------|----------|----------|----------| | [1]刘典等 | 18-44岁青年等待移植者 | 移植等待期不确定性 | 时间焦虑、生命悬置 | | [2]何雪松 | 成年ESRD患者（年龄未限定） | 疾痛叙事视角 | "过坎"的阶段性痛苦 | | [3]杨桃先等 | 围透析期患者（透析启动前3月至后6月） | 治疗过渡期 | 决策冲突、身份重构 | | [4]伍小燕 | 维持性血液透析（MHD）患者 | 长期治疗适应 | 持续性痛苦、社会疏离 | | [5]何宇彬等...

A binomial‑sum identity For a polynomial \(f(x)\) of degree \(\le n\), \[ \sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^k\binom{n}{k}f(k)=(-1)^n\Delta^n f(0), \] where \(\Delta\) is the forward difference operator. input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

metadata.solution: We consider two disjoint cases. \begin{itemize} \ii If $x$ is an integer, then it obviously works. Thus, we get $100$ solutions for $x \in \{1,2,\dots,100\}$. \ii Now suppose $x$ is not an integer, so we may set $n = \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor$ and $n+1 = \left\lceil x \right\rceil$. Then, the equation reads $ x^2 = n(n+1) \implies x = \sqrt{n(n+1)}. $ In other words, for each integer $n$, there is \emph{at most} one non-integer value of $x$ fulfilling the equation in the range $n < x < n+1$, namely $x = \sqrt{n(n+1)}$. On the other hand, the inequality $n < \sqrt{n(n+1)} < n+1$ is true for all $n \geq 1$. So each of the intervals $(1,2)$, $(2,3)$, \dots, $(99,100)$ does indeed have exactly one valid solution; namely, $x = \sqrt{n(n+1)}$ for the $n$th interval. \end{itemize} In total, we obtain $100 + 99 = \boxed{199}$ solutions.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 199, which is correct.

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: Given a $7$-tuple satisfying the given condition, let $S$ be the set of all of integers appearing at least once. Then the condition is equivalent to the tuple satisfying $a_i=i$ for all $i \in S$, because this means that $a_{a_i} = i$, so $a_{a_i} = a_i$. Now, we apply casework on $k = |S|$. There are $\binom{7}{k}$ ways to choose a subset of $S$ with fixed cardinality $k$, and there are $k^{7-k}$ ways to choose $a_j$ for $j \notin S$. Thus, \begin{align*} N &= \sum_{k=1}^{7} \binom{7}{k} k^{7-k} \\ &= 7 \cdot 1 + 21 \cdot 32 + 35 \cdot 81 + 35 \cdot 64 + 21 \cdot 25 + 7 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 1 \\ &= 7 + 672 + 2835 + 2240 + 525 + 42 + 1 = 6322. \end{align*} This gives the answer $\boxed{322}$.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 322, which is correct.

Structure of an idempotent function If \(y\) belongs to the image of \(f\), then \(y=f(x)\) for some \(x\). Idempotence gives \[ f(y)=f\bigl(f(x)\bigr)=f(x)=y, \] so every element of the image is a **fixed point** of \(f\). Conversely, if a function satisfies \(f(y)=y\) for every value \(y\) that appears as an image, then for any \(i\) \[ f\bigl(f(i)\bigr)=f(i), \] so the function is idempotent. Thus an idempotent map is completely determined by its set of fixed points. -------------------------------------------------------------------- ### 2. input: Let $\omega$ be a circle with center $O$ and radius $12$. Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of $\omega$. Let $H$ denote the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$. Compute the expected value of $OH^2$.

metadata.solution: \paragraph{First solution using complex numbers.} We first consider the analogous problem where $\omega$ has radius $1$ instead of $12$ (and will multiply the answer by $144$ to account for scaling at the end). Denote the points $A$, $B$, and $C$, by $a$, $b$, and $c$ in the complex plane, lying on the unit circle. It is well known that $H = a+b+c$; therefore, \begin{align*} OH^2 &= |a+b+c|^2 = (a+b+c)(\overline{a+b+c}) = (a+b+c)\left(\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} + \frac{1}{c}\right) \\ &= 3 + \frac ab + \frac ba + \frac bc + \frac cb + \frac ca + \frac ac. \end{align*} We now claim the six nonconstant terms each have expected value $0$. To see why this is true, observe that if we fix $b$, $\frac{a}{b}$ is just $a$ shifted counterclockwise by an angle of $\arg b$, so it still reflects a uniform point on the unit circle. For a fixed $a$, the number of possible values of $b$ is given by $\tau(a)$, the number of positive divisors of $a$. At each vertex the incident edges are paired – a rubber band enters the vertex along one edge and leaves along the other edge of the pair. Thus a placement is completely described by a *pairing* of the half‑edges at each vertex: * at a vertex of degree \(2k\) there are \((2k-1)!! = (2k-1)(2k-3)\cdots 3\cdot1\) possible pairings; * once all pairings are chosen the edges are automatically partitioned into disjoint closed walks (the rubber bands). input: Compute the smallest integer $k > 1$ such that there are exactly $10$ even integers $n \ge 2$ for which $k - n/2$ is divisible by $n$.

metadata.solution: Replace $n$ with $2m$ in what follows. Then $k - m$ is divisible by $2m$ when it equals $a \cdot 2m$ for some integer $a$; i.e.\ we require $k = (2a + 1) \cdot m$ for some integer $a$. In other words, $k$ needs to be an odd multiple of $m$. Thus, if $k = 2^e \cdot b$ for some integer $e \ge 0$ and odd integer $b$, then the number of $m$ for which $k$ is an odd multiple is given by the number of divisors of $b$. We thus can assume $e = 0$ and try to minimize $b$; we need to choose the minimal odd integer $b$ with $10$ positive divisors. Recall that the number of factors of a number is the product of one more than the exponents. Since $10 = 2 \cdot 5$, this means that either $k = p^9$ or $k = p^4 \cdot q$ for some primes $p$ and $q$. In the first case $k \ge 3^9$ and in the second case $k \ge 3^4 \cdot 5$; the smaller of these is $3^4 \cdot 5 = \boxed{405}$.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 405, which is correct.

input: There are $2025$ green pencils on a table. Every minute, Elphaba removes two randomly chosen pencils on the table. Right after that, Glinda adds back one pink pencil. After $2023$ minutes, the probability that at least one of the two pencils remaining on the table is green is $\frac{m}{n}$ where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute the remainder when $m+n$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: First, let's consider a particular green pencil $G_1$. The probability that $G_1$ survives all the way to the end is given by \begin{align*} \frac{2023}{2025} \cdot \frac{2022}{2024} \cdot \frac{2021}{2023} \cdot \cdots \cdot \frac{2}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{3} &= \frac{1}{2025} \cdot \frac{1}{2024} \cdot 2 \\ &= \frac{1}{1012 \cdot 2025}. \end{align*} However, note that at the end of the process, the number of green pencils remaining must be either $0$ or $1$. In other words, if $G_2$, \dots, $G_{2025}$ denote the remaining pencils, the event of $G_i$ surviving to the end is disjoint from any other such event. Hence, the probability is just $ \underbrace{ \frac{1}{1012 \cdot 2025} + \dots + \frac{1}{1012 \cdot 2025} }_{2025 \text{times}} = \frac{1}{1012}. $ The remainder when $1+1012$ is divided by $1000$ is $\boxed{013}$.

input: Compute the maximum possible value of $ab+bc+cd+de$ over all choices of positive integers $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, $e$ satisfying $a+b+c+d+e=60$.

metadata.solution: We show three solutions. \paragraph{First solution by inequalities.} Let $S$ denote the desired sum. The idea is to write $ S \coloneqq ab+bc+cd+de = (a+c+e)(b+d) - (ad+be). $ Note that \begin{itemize} \ii We have $ (a+c+e)(b+d) = (a+c+e)(60-(a+c+e)) = 900 - ((a+c+e)-30)^2. $ \ii We have $ad + be \ge (a+d-1) + (b+e-1) = 58 - c$. \end{itemize} Hence, we get an estimate of $ S \le 842 + \left( c - ((a+c+e)-30)^2 \right) . $ Now we consider cases on $c$. \begin{itemize} \ii If $c \le 28$, we get $S \le 842 + c \le 870$. Equality can indeed occur, for example if $a=e=1$, $c=28$, and $b+d=30$. \ii Now assume $c > 28$. Then $a+c+e > 30$, and from $a,e \ge 1$ we can make the estimate $ (a+c+e-30)^2 \ge (c-28)^2. $ Thus, we have $ S \le 842 + \left( c - (c-28)^2 \right). $ For every value of $c > 28$, it is easy to check $ c - (c- 28)^2 \le 28 $ so we have $c \le 870$ in this case too. And equality can indeed occur, for example if $a=e=1$, $c=29$, and $b+d=29$. \end{itemize} Hence we get a maximum of $\boxed{870}$...

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 870, which is correct.

input: Winston forgot the definition of a prime number. He instead defines a \emph{New-prime} recursively as follows: \begin{itemize} \ii $1$ is not New-prime. \ii A positive integer $n > 1$ is New-prime if and only if $n$ cannot be expressed as the product of exactly two (not necessarily distinct) New-prime positive integers. \end{itemize} Compute the number of positive integers dividing $5005^4$ which are New-primes.

metadata.solution: The main claim is: \begin{claim*} A positive integer is New-prime if and only if the sum of the exponents of its prime factors is odd. \end{claim*} \begin{proof} By induction on $n \ge 1$, with the base case given. Consider any $n$. If the sum of the exponents of $n$ is odd, then clearly it is not the product of two numbers whose sum of exponents is odd. Otherwise, if the sum of exponents of $n$ is even, consider any prime $p\mid n$. Then, $p$ and $\frac{n}{p}$ are both New-prime, since $\frac{n}{p}$ has an odd sum of exponents and is less than $n$. The induction applies, so this condition for New-primes works. \end{proof} Hence we need the number of divisors of $5005^4 = 5^4 \cdot 7^4 \cdot 11^4 \cdot 13^4$ with an odd exponent sum. This can be computed by using a roots of unity filter on $ P(X) \coloneqq (1 + X + X^2 + X^3 + X^4)^4 $ and looking for the sum of the coefficients of the terms with odd degree; that is $ \frac{P(1)-P(-1)}{2} = \frac{625-1}{2} = \boxed{312}. $

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 312, which is correct.

input: There is a unique triplet of integers $(a, b, c)$ such that $0 < a < b < c < 1000$ and $ \frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c=\frac{1}{315}. $ Compute $a$.

metadata.solution: The key observation is that $ \half \cdot \frac{1}{315} = \frac{3}{42 \cdot 45} = \frac{1}{42} - \frac{1}{45}. $ Then one can write the right-hand side as the sum of three Egyptian fractions as follows: \begin{align*} \half \cdot \frac{1}{315} = \frac{1}{42} - \frac{1}{45} &= \left( \frac{1}{42} - \frac{1}{43} \right) + \left( \frac{1}{43} - \frac{1}{44} \right) + \left( \frac{1}{44} - \frac{1}{45} \right) \\ &= \frac{1}{42 \cdot 43} + \frac{1}{43 \cdot 44} + \frac{1}{44 \cdot 45}. \end{align*} Doubling everything gives $ \frac{1}{315} = \frac{2}{42 \cdot 43} + \frac{2}{43 \cdot 44} + \frac{2}{44 \cdot 45}. $ Hence one can take $a = \frac{42 \cdot 43}{2}$, $b = \frac{43 \cdot 44}{2}$, $c = \frac{44 \cdot 45}{2}$. So $a = \boxed{903}$.

input: Alice has a deck of $2000$ cards, numbered $1$ through $2000$. Alice chooses an integer $1 \le n < 1000$ and deals Cheshire a random subset of $2n-1$ of the cards without repetition. Cheshire wins if the cards dealt contain any $n$ consecutively numbered cards. Compute the value of $n$ Alice should choose to minimize Cheshire's chances of winning.

metadata.solution: Fix a positive integer $n < 1000$. A subset of $2n - 1$ cards is called \emph{good} if it contains a collection of $n$ consecutively numbered cards. To enumerate the good subsets, we categorize them into buckets $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_{2000}$. Let $B_k$ be the set of all subsets $S$ of $2n - 1$ cards such that: $ k-1 \notin S \quad \text{and} \quad k, k+1, \dots, k+n-1 \in S. $ Note that every element of $B_k$ is a good subset, and every good subset is an element of some bucket $B_k$. Furthermore, every subset is part of at most $1$ bucket because a subset of $2n - 1$ cards cannot contain $2$ disjoint collections of $n$ consecutively numbered cards. Therefore the probability of Cheshire winning is given by: $ P_n = \frac {\lvert B_1 \rvert + \lvert B_2 \rvert + \dots + \lvert B_{2000} \rvert} {\binom{2000}{2n - 1}} $ By the definitions of $B_k$ we also have that: $ B_k = \begin{cases} \binom{2000 - n}{n - 1} & \text{if $k = 1$} \\ \binom{2000 - n - 1}{n - 1} & \text{if $2 \leq k \leq 2000-n$} \\ 0 & \text{if...

input: Let $ABC$ be a triangle with $\angle B = 60\dg$ and $AB = 8$. Let $D$ be the foot of the altitude from $A$ to $BC$, and let $M$ be the midpoint of $CD$. If $AM = BM$, compute $AC^2$.

metadata.solution: Since $\angle ABD = 60\dg$ and $\angle ADB = 90\dg$, it follows that $\triangle ABD$ is a $30\dg$-$60\dg$-$90\dg$ triangle. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); pair B = (0, 0); pair A = (4, 4sqrt(3)); pair C = (12, 0); pair D = (4, 0); pair M = (8, 0); pair G = (A + B + C)/3; filldraw(A--B--C--cycle, opacity(0.4)+palered, darkred+1); draw(A--M, darkred+1); draw(A--D, black); label("$8$", midpoint(A--B), dir(150)); dot("$A$", A, dir(A-G)); dot("$B$", B, dir(B-G)); dot("$C$", C, dir(C-G)); dot("$D$", D, dir(-90)); dot("$M$", M, dir(-90)); \end{asy} \end{center} This implies that $BD = 4$ and $AD = 4\sqrt{3}$. In addition, since $AM = BM$ and $\angle ABM = 60^\circ$, it follows that $\triangle ABM$ is equilateral. Thus $BM = AB = 8$, so it follows that $ DM = BM - BD = 4. $ Since $M$ is the midpoint of $CD$, we get that $CM = DM = 4$, so $CD = 8$. Now, we can use the Pythagorean Theorem on right triangle $\triangle ADC$ to get $ AC^2 = (4\sqrt{3})^2 + 8^2 = 64 + 48 = \boxed{112}. $

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 112, which is correct.

input: Let $P$ denote the product of all positive integers $n$ such that the least common multiple of $2n$ and $n^2$ is $62n-336$. Compute the remainder when $P$ is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: Note that when $n$ is even, $\opname{lcm} (2n,n^2) = n^2$, but when $n$ is odd, $\opname{lcm} (2n,n^2) = 2n^2$. We consider these two cases separately. \begin{itemize} \item If $n$ is even, then $\opname{lcm}(2n,n^2) = n^2 = 62n -336$. We can rearrange this to get $ n^2 - 62n + 336 = 0 $ which factors as $ (n-6)(n-56) = 0 $ so $n = 6$ and $n = 56$; and these both work because they are even. \item If $n$ is odd, then $\opname{lcm}(2n,n^2) = 2n^2 = 62n - 336$. This implies that $ 2n^2 - 62n + 336 = 0 \implies n^2 - 31n + 168 = 0. $ Factor to get $ (n-7)(n-24) = 0. $ However, $n=24$ is even, so the only solution in this case is $n = 7$, which works. \end{itemize} Multiplying all of the solutions gives $6\cdot 7 \cdot 56$, which is $2352$. Thus, $\boxed{352}$ is our answer.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 352, which is correct.

Therefore \[ L= \begin{cases} 2n^{2}, & n\text{ odd},\\[4pt] n^{2}, & n\text{ even}. \end{cases} \tag{3} \] -------------------------------------------------------------------- ### 2. input: Let $x$ be the unique positive real number satisfying $ 2^x+32^x=8^x+16^x. $ Compute $8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}$.

metadata.solution: Let $y = 2^x$. Note that our desired extraction $8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}$ can be written as $64y^3 - 64y = 64(y^3 - y)$. We can then write our given equation as $ y + y^5 = y^3 + y^4. $ Note that $y = 0, 1$ satisfy this equation so this factors as $ y(y-1)(y^3 - y - 1) = 0. $ Since $x > 0$, it follows that $y > 1$ and thus $y^3 - y = 1$. As such, the answer is $64(y^3 - y) = 64 \cdot 1 = \boxed{64}$ as desired.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 64, which is correct.

input: Compute the number of integers less than $1000$ which can be written in the form $ x^{\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor} + \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor^x $ for some positive real number $x$.

metadata.solution: Let $S = x^{\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor} + \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor^x$. We will use casework on $n \coloneqq \lfloor x\rfloor \ge 0$. In the edge case $n = 0$ (and $x \neq 0$), the sum has fixed value $S = 0^x + x^0 = 1$, which gives $1$ value. Now for a fixed choice of $n \ge 1$, the value of $S$ is strictly monotonically increasing in the interval $n \le x < n+1$. Hence the value of $S$ will range across $ 2n^n \le S < (n+1)^n + n^{n+1} $ for $n > 0$. In particular, if $n \ge 5$ then $S > 1000$. For $1 \le n \le 4$, we see the values of $S$ that appear are \begin{align*} n = 1 &\implies 2 \le S < 3 \\ n = 2 &\implies 8 \le S < 17 \\ n = 3 &\implies 54 \le S < 145 \\ n = 4 &\implies 512 \le S < 1649. \end{align*} Hence the total count is $1 + 1 + 9 + 91 + 488 = \boxed{590}$.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 590, which is correct.

input: Rosa the otter is stacking $53$ blocks in a tower. For $n \ge 1$, after successfully placing the previous $n-1$ blocks, the probability that placing the $n$\ts{th} block causes the whole tower to topple is $\frac{1}{54 - n}$. Compute the expected number of blocks placed successfully before the block that causes the tower to topple.

metadata.solution: Note that the tower must collapse after successfully placing $n$ blocks for some $1 \le n \le 53$ (as the last block guarantees a collapse). So the probability of the tower collapsing on placing the $n$\ts{th} block is $ \left(1 - \frac{1}{53}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{52}\right) \cdot \dotsm \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{55 - n} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{54 - n} = \frac{52}{53} \cdot \frac{51}{52} \dotsm \frac{54-n}{55-n} \cdot \frac{1}{54-n} = \frac{1}{53}. $ As such, each $1 \le n \le 53$ is equally likely. Thus, the expected number of blocks is thus $ \frac{0}{53} + \frac{1}{53} + \dots + \frac{52}{53} = \frac{52 \cdot 53}{2 \cdot 53} = \boxed{26}. $

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 26, which is correct.

model_graded.answer: The probability that the tower topples on the \(n^{\text{th}}\) block is \[ p_n=\frac{1}{54-n},\qquad n=1,2,\dots ,53, \] and the probability that it does **not** topple on the \(n^{\text{th}}\) block, given that it is still standing, is \[ q_n=1-p_n=\frac{53-n}{54-n}. \] Let \(N\) be the random step at which the tower topples. For \(n\ge 1\), \[ \Pr(N=n)=p_n\prod_{i=1}^{n-1}q_i. \] The product telescopes: \[ \prod_{i=1}^{n-1}q_i =\prod_{i=1}^{n-1}\frac{53-i}{54-i} =\frac{53-(n-1)}{53} =\frac{54-n}{53}. \] Hence \[ \Pr(N=n)=\frac{1}{54-n}\cdot\frac{54-n}{53}=\frac1{53}, \qquad n=1,2,\dots ,53. \] Thus \(N\) is uniformly distributed on \(\{1,2,\dots ,53\}\). The number of blocks placed **successfully** before the toppling block is \(N-1\). Since \(N\) is uniform, \[ \mathbb{E}[N-1]=\frac{0+1+2+\cdots+52}{53} =\frac{1}{53}\cdot\frac{52\cdot53}{2} =26. \] \[ \boxed{26} \] Thus the expected number of successfully placed blocks before the tower topples is **26**. ANSWER: 26
model_graded.explanation: The grader model responded with: CORRECT

input: Compute the number of functions $f \colon \{ 1, 2, \dots, 15 \} \to \{-1, 0, 1\}$ such that $f(ab) = f(a)f(b)$ holds whenever $a$ and $b$ are positive integers with $ab \le 15$.

metadata.solution: Since $f(1)^2 = f(1)$, we have either $f(1) = 0$ or $f(1) = 1$. \begin{itemize} \ii If $f(1) = 0$ then we deduce $f \equiv 0$, which certainly works. \ii If $f(1) = 1$, then the function is uniquely determined by its values on $f(2)$, $f(3)$, $f(5)$, $f(7)$, $f(11)$, $f(13)$. There are thus $3^6 = 729$ functions in this case. \end{itemize} Hence the answer $1 + 729 = \boxed{730}$.

model_extracted_exact_match.explanation: The model answered 730, which is correct.

model_graded.answer: The condition forces \(f\) to be a multiplicative map. - For any \(a\) we have \(f(a)=f(a)f(1)\). Hence either \(f(1)=0\) (which forces \(f\equiv0\)) or \(f(1)=1\). - If \(f(1)=1\) then the value on any integer is determined by its prime factors. For a prime \(p\le 15\) we may choose \(f(p)\in\{-1,0,1\}\). If a prime is assigned \(0\) then every number divisible by that prime must map to \(0\). If a prime is assigned \(\pm1\) then all numbers built from it receive the product of those signs (with the usual rules for exponents). Since each prime can be chosen independently, there are \(3^{\pi(15)}\) choices for the values on the primes, where \(\pi(15)=6\) (the primes are \(2,3,5,7,11,13\)). This gives \(3^{6}=729\) functions with \(f(1)=1\). Adding the unique zero‑map (where \(f(1)=0\) and all values are \(0\)) gives \[ \boxed{730} \]

Recognize a forward difference For any function \(f\) define the forward difference \[ \Delta f(k)=f(k+1)-f(k). \] By induction one shows \[ \Delta^{n}f(0)=\sum_{j=0}^{n}(-1)^{\,n-j}\binom{n}{j}f(j) \qquad (n\ge 0). \] Hence \[ \sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^k\binom{n}{k}f(k)=(-1)^n\Delta^{n}f(0). \] Taking \(n=717\) and \(f(k)=(6+239k)^{717}\) we obtain \[ S=(-1)^{717}\Delta^{717}f(0)=-\Delta^{717}f(0). \] --- ### 2. The \(n\)‑th forward difference of a polynomial If \(p(k)=a_nk^n+\text{(lower terms)}\) is a polynomial of degree \(n\), then each forward difference lowers the degree by one and multiplies the leading coefficient by the current degree. input: The incircle of $ABC$ is tangent to $BC$ at $D$. Let the internal bisectors of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle BDA$ meet at $I_B$ and their external bisectors at $E_B$, and define $I_C$ and $E_C$ similarly. Suppose that $I_BI_C=1$, $E_BE_C=6$, and the area of quadrilateral $I_BI_CE_BE_C$ is $7$. The area of triangle $ABC$ can be written as $\tfrac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

metadata.solution: Let $a \coloneq BC$, $b \coloneq CA$, $c \coloneq AB$ and $s \coloneq \half (a+b+c)$. Let $d = AD$. Hence $BD = s-b$ and $CD = s-c$. We start with the following claim. \begin{claim*} The incircles of $\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle ACD$ (centered at $I_B$ and $I_C$) are tangent at a point $T_I$ on line $AD$. Similarly, the excircles (opposite $D$, centered at $E_B$ and $E_C$) are tangent at a point $T_E$ on line $AD$. Moreover, $T_I$ and $T_E$ are reflections across the midpoint of $AD$. \end{claim*} \begin{proof} The length of the tangents from $A$ along line $AD$ are given by $ \frac{c+d+(s-b)}{2}-(s-b) = \frac{b+d+(s-c)}{2}-(s-c) $ and hence the tangency points coincide at the point $\frac{d+(s-a)}{2}$ from $A$. input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

metadata.solution: We'll use $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$ for the four suits and $1$, $2$, $3$, $4$ for the four ranks. Hence we need to fill the grid with these $16$ cards which come in four ranks and four suits, each pair once from $a_1$ to $d_4$, such that adjacent cards either share a rank or share a suit. In fact, even for a $2 \times 2$ sub-square, there are not that many possible shapes that can appear. We claim that, up to relabeling the suits and ranks, only four ``kinds'' of shapes could appear, which we abbreviate with the following names: \begin{description} \item[Flush] $\left[ \begin{smallmatrix} a_1 & a_2 \\ a_3 & a_4 \end{smallmatrix} \right]$ (i.e.\ all suits the same) \item[Four-of-a-kind] $\left[ \begin{smallmatrix} a_1 & b_1 \\ c_1 & d_1 \end{smallmatrix} \right]$ (i.e.\ all ranks the same) \item[Horizontal-pairs] $\left[ \begin{smallmatrix} a_1 & b_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 \end{smallmatrix} \right]$ (i.e.\ two ranks and two suits, same ranks horizontally) \item[Vertical-pairs] $\left[ \begin{smallmatrix} a_1 & a_2 \\ b_1 &...
